selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm going to be straight with you because that's how I've always been. I don't have social media and honestly, I think that's the best way to deal with fake content right there. Just don't participate in it. I'm seventy-four years old, I've been fixing engines and transmissions for decades, and I can tell you that sometimes the simplest solution is the right one. When something's broken beyond repair, you don't keep tinkering with it, you just walk away. That's social media for most people. I see folks at the shop, younger mechanics mostly, glued to their phones during breaks, getting worked up over some nonsense they read online. Half of it's made up, the other half is twisted around to make you angry. What's the point? When I want real information, I talk to actual people face to face. That's how I've always done things. In my line of work, you can't afford to believe everything you hear anyway. Someone tells you they just need an oil change, you still pop the hood and check for yourself. You verify things. You use your own judgment. The problem with all these social media platforms is they've made people lazy about thinking. They see a headline, they react, they share it without checking if it's even true. Back when I was coming up, my parents both had good educations and they taught me to question things, even if I didn't go the university route myself. Common sense goes a long way. If something sounds too outrageous or too perfect, it probably is. My advice? Spend less time online and more time in the real world. Go for a run, read an actual book, talk to your neighbors. I do all those things and I'm not missing out on anything important. The people who need to reach me know where to find me. Everything else is just noise, and life's too short to fill your head with garbage you can't trust anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 74-year-old woman who has no social media presence, which is the most defining characteristic for this topic. Her high extraversion suggests she values direct, face-to-face communication. Low agreeableness indicates she'll be blunt and direct in her opinions, not sugar-coating her views. Low openness means she prefers traditional methods and is skeptical of new technologies. Her occupation as a mechanic provides practical, hands-on perspective. Her depression and widowed status might contribute to a somewhat cynical or world-weary tone. Low conscientiousness suggests a more casual, less structured approach to the response. Her hobbies (hiking, cycling, reading, running) all involve offline activities, reinforcing her disconnection from social media. Her parents' education levels suggest she values critical thinking despite her own vocational path.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, skeptical, practical, no-nonsense, slightly cynical but grounded in real-world experience
